topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation arrived on thick, cream-colored cardstock, the kind usually reserved for significant life announcements. Inside, the script was elegant, almost clinical: "Dr. Elias Vance, Licensed Clinical Psychologist. Schedule your initial consultation." My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. Elias. My childhood tormentor. The boy who had once delighted in tripping me in the hallway and whispering cruel things until I felt small enough to disappear. Now, he was offering me therapy. I stared at the embossed seal of the practice, my jaw tight. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, and I gripped the edges of the paper until my knuckles were white. *What is he trying to do?* I thought, the question sharp and brittle in my mind. The thought of sitting across from him, under the pretense of vulnerability, made my palms sweat. I wanted to lash out, to scream that this was a sick joke, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a suffocating sense of betrayal.

[story 2]

When Elias finally called, his voice was measured, calm, utterly devoid of the sharp mockery I remembered so vividly. "Hello, [Character's Name]. I received your inquiry. I look forward to our session." A strange, unsettling stillness settled over me. I paced my cramped living room, running a hand through my hair repeatedly. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming protest. I remember the sheer, unadulterated antagonism of those years—the way his smirk used to twist his face. Now, he was offering expertise. It felt like a calculated maneuver, a performance designed to elicit some reaction. I leaned against the wall, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to project an indifference I absolutely did not feel. My breath hitched slightly as I listened to his smooth articulation, a tightness building behind my eyes. *He thinks this is funny. He thinks he can just flip the script.* I wanted to tell him, right then and there, that he was entirely wrong, that his past actions held no relevance to this supposed professional encounter.

[story 3]

I reread the introductory email three times. Each time, the same internal tremor ran through me—a restless, agitated energy that made my limbs feel heavy and clumsy. Elias Vance. The name itself was a physical irritant. I remember the sting of his words, the way he dismantled my confidence piece by piece. To now have him positioned as an authority figure, someone tasked with dissecting my psyche, felt like a profound insult to the memory of our shared history. I stood in front of the mirror, scrutinizing my reflection. My expression was a mask of strained neutrality, but inside, a furious churning began. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the tension radiating outward. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my heart. *He thinks he can just step into this role?* The sheer audacity of it made my throat constrict, and I felt a desperate need to find a way to dismantle this charade, to show him exactly how misplaced his current position was.
